title: Why How Creators in Ghana Get Paid Online Is Costing You More Than You Think
author: Unchained Commerce
excerpt: Selling digital products from Nigeria, Ghana, Kenya, Pakistan, or Bangladesh is possible with crypto-native infrastructure that has no geographic restrictions.

content.text: In Ghana, digital creators who sell ebooks, courses, or software are often blocked by PayPal and Stripe. Every day, more are waking up to their payments stuck in a limbo, their store profits dwindling. Don't be another one to fall for this trap. The cost of getting this wrong is more than just financial loss; it's your reputation, your time, and the trust of your customers.
The Mistake Almost Every Creator Makes First

Ghana is not alone in this struggle. Many creators in Nigeria, Kenya, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Ethiopia, and other restricted countries fall prey to the limitations of traditional payment processors. They mistakenly assume it's just a minor inconvenience or that they'll eventually find a workaround. But the truth is, these platforms have a track record of freezing or seizing accounts without warning, leaving creators in the dark and their customers frustrated.
Why This Keeps Happening to Good Stores

The root of this problem lies in the stringent Know-Your-Customer (KYC) requirements enforced by mainstream payment processors. These requirements often lead to account restrictions, and in some cases, outright closures. This not only affects creators in restricted countries but also those with limited bank accounts or unverified identities. The result is a store that cannot collect payments or worse, a store that's been taken down altogether.
The Real Cost of Getting This Wrong

The real cost of getting this wrong goes beyond financial loss. It's the loss of customer trust, the time spent trying to resolve the issue, and the damage to your reputation. In today's digital age, where competition is fierce, creators can ill afford to have their store operations hampered by unnecessary restrictions. The cost of getting this wrong is not just financial; it's your livelihood.
The blockchain has no map of restricted countries — a wallet in Lagos processes identically to one in London.

Geographic neutrality is not a feature of crypto payments — it is the fundamental property of how blockchains work.
